MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS CHINATOWN COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT CENTER, INC., HAMLIN HOTEL 2019, LP'S DEMURRER to COMPLAINT

Continued for hearing to November 13, 2025.

Plaintiff Lataya Robinson obtained leave of the court to file a first amended complaint that was attached as Exhibit A to the Vannucci Declaration filed on July 11, 2025 ("Original FAC"). When Robinson filed it, the clerk rejected it nearly a month later because party names were listed as "et al." (Vannucci Decl., 10/8/25, Ex. C.) Robinson submitted a new version of a pleading styled as a first amended complaint ("Filed FAC") on October 3, 2025. (Id., para. 6.) The court clerk accepted it for filing and backdated it to September 29, 2025. (Id.)

The court's job is to construe the pleadings to do substantial justice among the parties. (See Code Civ. Proc., section 452.) That task is complicated here by the delay in processing the filing and by the coming trial, set for November 17, 2025. The court has carefully compared the Original FAC and the Filed FAC and it appears that Robinson's changes merely responded to the clerk's rejection notice, and the demurrers and motions to strike filed by the parties and directed at the Original FAC are equally applicable to the Filed FAC. The court declines to strike the Filed FAC. It deems the demurrers and motions to strike filed on September 23, 2025, and September 29, 2025, as directed at the Filed FAC. It continues the demurrers and motions to strike to November 13, 2025, at 9 a.m. in Department 301. Off Calendar Aug-04-2025 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Atty. John Fitzpatrick Vannucci, Esq. of Law Offices of John Fitzpatrick Vannucci (tel: 415-981-7500) for the Plaintiff Lataya Robinson.
Atty. Jennifer Gelini, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of Bennett, Gelini & Gelini (tel: 510-444-7688) for Defendants Pedro's Moving Services, Inc.
Atty. Caroline Rogers, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of Hawkins Parnell & Young LLP (tel: 415-979-2012) for Defendant Caritas Management Corporation.
Atty. Julia Aguilera, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of Van De Poel, Levy, Thomas LLP (tel: 925-934-6102) for Defendant AmOne Corp.
Atty. Kevin Cholakian, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of Cholakian & Associates (tel: 650-871-9544) for Defendants Chinatown Community Development Center, Inc. and Hamlin Hotel 2019, LP.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF LATAYA ROBINSON's Motion To Amend To Include Punitive Damages.

Matter argued and submitted. Upon conclusion, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Lataya Robinson's motion to amend to include punitive damages allegations is granted.

Robinson was a tenant of the Hamlin Hotel. She sues for injuries she suffered when an elevator ceiling fell on her head at the Hamlin Hotel. The Hamlin Hotel is owned and operated by Defendants Chinatown Community Center and Hamlin Hotel 2019, LP, and was managed by Defendant Caritas Management Corporation. Robinson alleges that the Hamlin Hotel was controlled at the time by Defendant AmOne Corp, the manager of a construction project taking place at the site.

The court excuses Robinson's late filing. It does not appear that the defendants were unable to adequately oppose the motion in the time they had.

Leave to amend is liberally granted. (See, e.g., Morgan v. Superior Court (1959) 172 Cal.App.2d 527, 530 ["it is a rare case in which a court will be justified in refusing a party leave to amend his pleadings so that he may properly present his case"] [simplified].) Generally, the only basis to deny a plausible amendment is where there is prejudice.

The trial is presently set for November 2025 and Robinson's motion to amend to add these allegations was denied without prejudice in January 2025 until she secured a trial continuance, which she has done. The court does not see prejudice in the inability to move for summary judgment on the punitive damages allegations; as Robinson notes, none of the cases the defendants rely on so hold. (See, e.g., Morgan, supra, 172 Cal.App.2d at p. 530 [trial court abused discretion in denying amendment before divorce action was set for trial]; Mabie v. Hyatt (1998) 61 Cal.App.4th 581, 595-596 [amendment discussion is dicta, but the case explains that attorneys should file the claim they have evidence for and then conduct discovery and seek leave to amend later to add new claims to avoid a malicious prosecution action for filing unsupported claims]; Kittredge Sports Co. v. Superior Court (1989) 213 Cal.App.3d 1045, 1048 [reversing trial court order denying leave to amend four years into personal injury lawsuit, notwithstanding defendant's claim of unreasonable delay]; Atkinson v. Elk Corp. (2003) 109 Cal.App.4th 739, 760 [trial court should have allowed amendment during trial].) Nor does delay alone constitute prejudice. (Kittredge Sports, supra, 213 Cal.App.3d at p. 1048.)

AmOne Corp. challenges the sufficiency of the pleading, but its concerns may be raised on demurrer. (Atkinson, supra, 109 Cal.App.4th at p. 760 ["we believe that the better course of action would have been to allow Atkinson to amend the complaint and then let the parties test its legal sufficiency in other appropriate proceedings"].)

Order submitted.

Off Calendar Feb-07-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Atty. MICHAEL PADWAY, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of Michael Padway & Associates (tel: 510-839-5900) and Atty. JOHN VANUCCI, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of Law Offices of John Fitzpatrick Vannucci (tel: 415-981-5700) for the Plaintiff.
Atty. KEVIN CHOLAKIAN, Esq., Atty. CARMEN GUTMAN, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of CHOLAKIAN AND ASSOCIATES (tel: 650-871-9544) appearing along with Atty. RENA SERA, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of Tyson & Mendes LLP (tel: 628-253-5070) for Defendants Chinatown Community Development Center, Inc. and Hamlin Hotel 2019, LP.
Atty. JENNIFER GELINI, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of Bennett, Gelini & Gelini (tel: 510-444-7688) for Defendant Pedro's Moving Services, Inc.
Atty. MALCOLM DONALDSON, Esq. of Hugo Parker LLP (tel: 415-808-0333) for Caritas Management Corporation.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT CHINATOWN COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT CENTER, INC., AND HAMLIN HOTEL 2019, LP's Motion To Compel Compliance With Court Order And For Sanctions Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure Sections 2023.010, 2023.030 And 2032.240.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling with modifications:

Defendant's motion is denied.

In October 2023, defendant Pedro's Moving Services, Inc. moved to compel plaintiff Lataya Robinson to appear for a psychological independent medical examination. The moving papers identified "the time, place, manner, conditions, scope, and nature of the examination, as well as the identity and the specialty, if any, of the person or persons who will perform the examination," as required under CCP 2032.310(b). This court granted the motion by order of November 9, 2023.

Subsequently, defendants were required by events to obtain a new examiner, as set out in the moving declaration of Carmen Gutman. Defendants Chinatown Community Development Center and Hamlin Hotel 2019 LP (collectively CCDC) now seek an order compelling Robinson's compliance with the November 9 order. The parties have met and conferred; Robinson's basis for objecting is exclusively that the November 9 order is no longer effective because it was based on an order for a particular examination that defendants can no longer offer.

The discovery statutes are intended to be liberally construed to give access to information. But this court cannot supersede an express statutory provision of the Discovery Act in order to effectuate this purpose. CCP 2032.310(b) requires a moving party to state, inter alia, the identity of the person to perform the examination. CCP 2032.320(d) states that "[a]n order granting a physical or mental examination shall specify the person or persons who may perform the examination." The November 9 order did not do so, but it did state at page 1, line 25 that the examination was ordered "as requested" in the moving papers. It is best construed as ordering Robinson to submit to an examination by the particular examiners identified in the October 2023 moving papers; any other construction would violate 2032.320(d)'s requirement that the order identify the examiner. Because CCDC does not seek compliance with all of the statutorily-required terms of a previous order, its motion is denied.

The court grants Robinson's request to file her opposition late in light of the CCP 473(b) declaration she has provided.

The court finds that CCDC has acted with substantial justification in bringing this motion and does not award sanctions to Robinson.

Counsel for the Plaintiff is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling with a cover letter reflecting compliance with CRC 3.1312.

DEFENDANT AMONE CORP. ERRONEOUSLY SUED AS AMONE CORPORATION MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR ALTERNATIVELY, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (PART 2 OF 2 FOR PURPOSES OF ENTRY OF TENTATIVE RULING)
Off Calendar Jan-21-2025 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or Alternatively, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Jan-21-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT AMONE CORP.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or Alternatively, Summary Adjudication.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant AmOne's motion for summary judgment and motion for summary adjudication on the first cause of action for premises liability/negligence are denied and its motion for summary adjudication on the second cause of action for willful failure to warn is granted.

Defendant failed to maintain its initial summary motion burden as to the first cause of action. Defendant argues that it contractually assumed only one duty as to the elevator-"to remove the current elevator and install a new elevator in its place." (UMF 14.) To support this assertion, the defendant proffers the Standard Form of Agreement Between Owner and Contractor Document. (Defendant's Ex. 5.) Article I of that agreement provides that: "The Contract Documents consist of this modified AIA Document A102-2017 Agreement ("Agreement"), the Exhibits to this Agreement, Conditions of the Contract (General, Supplementary, and other Conditions, as modified." Defendant only attached as exhibit 5 the Standard Form of Agreement Between Owner and Contractor Document. None of the other cited documents (including the referenced General Conditions) are attached and there is no clear evidence that the Standard Form of Agreement Between Owner and Contractor Document constitutes the entire agreement. (See Wong Decl., par. 6b.) It is therefore unclear exactly what contractual duties the defendant owed and thus it failed to maintain its burden.

The court notes that the plaintiff proffers a copy of General Conditions and Article 10 of those conditions show a duty to provide safety conditions. (Plaintiff's Ex. 3.) The court sustains the defendant's objection to exhibit 3 based on lack of foundation and authentication since plaintiff's counsel Padway proffers the General Conditions and he lacks personal knowledge regarding the terms of defendant's agreement. (Padway Decl., par. 4.) In any event, the defendant failed to maintain its burden. As an aside, evidence indicates that the defendant was aware that the ceiling tiles were sitting unattached prior to the accident. (Plaintiff's Ex. 1 [Wong Depo., 57:13-58:23].)

The second cause of action for willful failure to warn is assertedly based on Civil Code section 846. That code section provides that the immunity to property owners when they open up their property for recreational purposesdoes not apply where there is a willful failure to warn. This theory of liability is inapposite in this case.

Order signed.
